trials.supervisor_analysis: Baseline established at score 50.0 (0.83ms runtime, 0.975x vs reference). GEMM dominates at 95%. TF32 provides major speedup. Advancing to stage 2 with 18 minutes remaining for one focused optimization attempt.

nudges.text: You've spent over 5 minutes reading files and setting up a todo list but haven't run the benchmark yet. Please run `/opt/venv/bin/python3 /workspace/test_harness.py --level 3 --problem-id 33` immediately to establish your baseline score before doing anything else.

You have spent over 10 minutes without running the benchmark and the same steps keep repeating. Stop all file reading and planning. Run the benchmark command RIGHT NOW: `/opt/venv/bin/python3 /workspace/test_harness.py --level 3 --problem-id 33`. You are running out of time.

The 300s shell timeout is too short for `torch.compile` first-run on AMD (5-15 min is normal). Check if your `generated_kernel.py` uses `torch.compile` — if so, remove it and start with a simple PyTorch-based `ModelNew` that just replicates the reference forward pass using the same `nn.Linear` layers. This will establish a baseline score quickly, and you can optimize from there.

For batch_size=8 with these dimensions, Triton GEMM kernels will likely be slower than rocBLAS. Instead, keep `torch.mm`/`F.linear` for the matmuls and write a small Triton kernel to fuse just the pointwise ops (bias add + tanh, or concatenation). Pointwise Triton kernels compile in seconds, not minutes.

To avoid the `torch.cat` overhead, split `i2h.weight` into two parts: `w_x = i2h.weight[:, :input_size]` and `w_h = i2h.weight[:, input_size:]`, then compute `x @ w_x.T + hidden @ w_h.T + i2h.bias` using two separate matmuls. This eliminates the concatenation memory allocation entirely. Combine that with a small Triton kernel fusing the bias add + tanh.

A score of 60 (1.001x speedup) is essentially just the baseline — don't finalize yet. You haven't tried `torch.compile` with TF32 enabled together. The earlier torch.compile attempt was without TF32. Also, the split-weight numerical mismatch can be fixed: two matmuls summed differs from one concatenated matmul only by float rounding, so check if the test harness uses a tolerance — you may just need slightly different accumulation order, or use `torch.addmm` for fused matmul+bias.

You're stuck at score 60 re-running the same code. Try these concrete changes: (1) Re-attempt the split-weight approach — the test harness likely uses `torch.allclose` with a tolerance, so small FP rounding differences may pass; check the full test output for the tolerance used. (2) Use `torch.addmm(bias, x, w_x.T)` which fuses bias+matmul in one kernel call. (3) Pre-transpose the weight matrices in `__init__` to avoid transposition overhead each forward call. Stop re-running the same 60-score implementation.

Try `torch.compile(forward_fn, mode="reduce-overhead")` combined with TF32 — `reduce-overhead` enables CUDA graphs which eliminates kernel launch overhead across the 4-5 kernels in forward. Also try wrapping just the forward logic (not the whole module) with `torch.compile`. Your earlier `torch.compile` attempt used `mode="default"` without TF32 — the combination hasn't been tested yet.

Try `torch.compile` with `mode="default"` and TF32 together — your earlier compile attempt was without TF32, and your reduce-overhead attempt failed because of the manual Triton kernel. Use pure PyTorch ops (no manual Triton kernel) and let `torch.compile` handle the fusion. The compile will fuse bias+tanh automatically into one kernel.
